title: Work & Future Plans

vocab.career: the series of jobs that a person has during their working life
vocab.qualification: an official document showing you have passed an exam or completed a course
vocab.experience: knowledge or skill gained from doing a job or activity
vocab.ambition: a strong desire to achieve something
vocab.internship: a period of work experience offered by an employer for a limited time

phrases: On the one hand... on the other hand...

questions: What job would you like to do in the future?
Do you think it is important to go to university?
What skills do you think employers want?
Have you ever had a part-time job?
What would be your ideal working environment?
answers: I would say I would like to work in software engineering because I enjoy solving problems and building things. I am also interested in how technology can improve people's lives.
I think university is important for some careers, like medicine or law, but not for everything. Practical experience can sometimes be more valuable than a degree.
Employers want communication skills, teamwork, and reliability. They also value people who are willing to learn and adapt to new situations.
Yes, I worked in a cafe last summer. It taught me how to deal with customers and manage my time better.
My ideal working environment would be flexible and friendly. I would prefer a mix of working from home and meeting colleagues in person.

part2.question: What is more important when choosing a job: money or job satisfaction?
part2.photoA: A person looking happy while working in a creative studio
part2.photoB: A person in a formal office with a high salary but looking stressed
part2.model: In photo A, the person seems to enjoy their work in a creative environment. In photo B, the person probably earns more but looks stressed and unhappy. I think job satisfaction is more important in the long term because money cannot fix burnout or unhappiness. However, everyone needs enough money to live comfortably. I would say the ideal job balances both: fair pay and work you actually enjoy.

part3.task: A school is designing a programme to help students prepare for work. Discuss the ideas and decide which two would be most useful.
part3.model:
A: I think work experience placements are the most useful because students see what real jobs are like.
B: That's a good point, but they can be difficult to organise for everyone.
A: True. Career talks from professionals are easier to arrange and expose students to many different jobs.
B: I agree. And interview practice would help students feel confident when they actually apply for jobs.
A: So let's choose career talks and interview practice.
B: Yes, I agree with you.

questions: Is it better to have one career or change jobs many times?
Should work-life balance be more important than salary?
How will technology change jobs in the future?
What advice would you give someone choosing a career?
answers: I think changing jobs can be positive because it gives you new experiences and skills. However, staying in one career can lead to deeper expertise and promotions.
I believe work-life balance is crucial. A high salary is nice, but it means nothing if you are too stressed or tired to enjoy life.
Technology will automate many routine jobs, so people will need to learn new skills. Jobs that require creativity and emotional intelligence will probably grow.
I would advise them to choose something they are genuinely interested in, not just what pays well. Passion usually leads to better long-term success.

homework.reading: Read a job advertisement for a position you might like. Identify the qualifications and skills required.
homework.listening: Listen to a podcast about future careers. Note down two jobs that may disappear and two that may grow.
homework.writing: Write an essay (140–190 words): 'Should young people choose a career based on passion or salary?'
